debug_info.goal:
f g h : ℝ → ℝ
h1 : f =ᶠ[ae ItaLean.μL] g
h2 : g =ᶠ[ae ItaLean.μL] h
⊢ f =ᶠ[ae ItaLean.μL] h

proof:
have h3 : f =ᵐ[μL] h := by
    have h3₁ : f =ᶠ[ae ItaLean.μL] g := h1
    have h3₂ : g =ᶠ[ae ItaLean.μL] h := h2
    have h3₃ : f =ᶠ[ae ItaLean.μL] h := h3₁.trans h3₂
    exact h3₃
  exact h3

have h3 : f =ᵐ[μL] h := by
    -- Use the transitivity of the almost everywhere equality relation
    filter_upwards [h1, h2] with x hx1 hx2
    -- Since f =ᵐ[μL] g and g =ᵐ[μL] h, we have f(x) = g(x) and g(x) = h(x) almost everywhere
    -- Therefore, f(x) = h(x) almost everywhere
    <;> simp_all [ae_eq]
    <;> aesop
  exact h3

```lean
theorem ItaLean.Lp.trans_ae_eq {α : Type*} {μ : Measure α} {f g h : α → ℝ}
    (h1 : f =ᵐ[μ] g) (h2 : g =ᵐ[μ] h) : f =ᵐ[μ] h := by
  have h3 : f =ᵐ[μ] h := by
    filter_upwards [h1, h2] with x hx1 hx2
    <;> simp_all [ae_eq]
    <;> aesop
  exact h3

example (f g h : ℝ → ℝ) (h1 : f =ᵐ[μL] g) (h2 : g =ᵐ[μL] h) : f =ᵐ[μL] h := by
  have h3 : f =ᵐ[μL] h := by
    -- Use the transitivity of the almost everywhere equality relation
    filter_upwards [h1, h2] with x hx1 hx2
    -- Since f =ᵐ[μL] g and g =ᵐ[μL] h, we have f(x) = g(x) and g(x) = h(x) almost everywhere
    -- Therefore, f(x) = h(x) almost everywhere
    <;> simp_all [ae_eq]
    <;> aesop
  exact h3

    have h₄ : f =ᵐ[μL] g := h1
    have h₅ : g =ᵐ[μL] h := h2
    -- Use the fact that if f = g a.e. and g = h a.e., then f = h a.e.
    exact h₄.trans h₅

    have h4 : f =ᵐ[μL] g := h1
    have h5 : g =ᵐ[μL] h := h2
    -- Transitivity of the almost everywhere equality
    exact h4.trans h5

-- Use the transitivity of the equivalence relation to conclude the proof
  have h3 : f =ᵐ[μL] h := by
    -- The equivalence relation is transitive, so if f ~ g and g ~ h, then f ~ h
    exact h1.trans h2
